name: Head-Shifting (Two-Phase)
summary: Without a dummy node: first shift the head pointer past any leading matches (Phase 1), then run the same look-ahead/bypass loop on the rest (Phase 2). Shows exactly the head edge-case the dummy sentinel is designed to remove.
visualizationIntent: init: values = input nodes (NO dummy) with sequential arrows; links = next-index array; start pointer on index 0. phase1-loop (line 8): while the head node itself matches val, mark it removed (red) and slide start forward to links[start] — this is the special head-handling the dummy approach avoids. check (line 11): if start reached -1 (list emptied) short-circuit to an empty result. phase2-loop (line 15): current walks from start; read nxt = current.next. update-remove (match): reroute current past the matched node (changed_links fires), mark it removed (red). move (no match): current glides onto nxt. return: walk from start and show result as the cleaned value list.

lineExplanations.3: Use the input values directly as the node list — this approach has no dummy sentinel.
lineExplanations.4: Build the next-pointer array: node i points to node i+1, and the last node points to -1 (null).
lineExplanations.5: A per-node flag array — a 1 marks a deleted node (drives the red styling); it doesn't affect the algorithm.
lineExplanations.6: Will hold the arrow rerouted this step so the renderer can flash it.
lineExplanations.7: start marks the current head node (index 0), or -1 if the list is empty.
lineExplanations.8: Phase 1 — while the head node itself matches val, it has to be removed from the front.
lineExplanations.9: Mark this matching head node as removed (red).
lineExplanations.10: Slide the head forward to the next node — the old head is dropped from the list.
lineExplanations.11: Did Phase 1 consume the entire list (start ran off the end)?
lineExplanations.12: If so, the answer is the empty list…
lineExplanations.13: …return it immediately — there is nothing left to scan.
lineExplanations.14: Phase 2 — begin the walking pointer at the now-safe head; from here we inspect current's next node.
lineExplanations.15: Keep looping while current still has a next node.
lineExplanations.16: Look at the candidate: nxt is the node directly after current.
lineExplanations.17: Does the candidate node hold the value we're removing?
lineExplanations.18: Delete it: reroute current's next-pointer past the candidate to whatever came after it.
lineExplanations.19: Detach the removed node's own next-pointer so it dangles off the live list.
lineExplanations.20: Mark the candidate as removed — the renderer turns it red.
lineExplanations.21: Record that current's arrow just changed, so it flashes this step.
lineExplanations.23: The candidate survives, so advance current onto it.
lineExplanations.24: No arrow changed this step, so clear the flash marker.
lineExplanations.25: Prepare an empty list to collect the survivors.
lineExplanations.26: start is the new head after Phase 1 — begin the final walk there.
lineExplanations.27: Walk until we run off the end of the surviving chain (-1).
lineExplanations.29: Follow the next-pointer to the next surviving node.
lineExplanations.30: Return the cleaned list of values.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: declares the class wrapper the platform runs.
syntaxExplanations.2: def removeElements(self, head, val): defines the method; head is the input list of node values, val is the value to remove.
syntaxExplanations.3: = binds values to the same list object as head.
syntaxExplanations.4: range(1, len(values)) yields 1,2,…,n-1; list(...) collects them; + [-1] appends a final -1.
syntaxExplanations.5: [0] * len(values) makes a list of len(values) zeros.
syntaxExplanations.7: 0 if len(values) > 0 else -1 is a conditional expression: the value before if when the test holds, otherwise the value after else.
syntaxExplanations.8: and requires BOTH conditions to be true; it short-circuits, so values[start] is only read when start != -1.
syntaxExplanations.9: Stores 1 into removed at index start.
syntaxExplanations.10: links[start] reads the element of links at index start.
syntaxExplanations.11: if … : runs its block only when the condition is true; == tests equality.
syntaxExplanations.12: Creates a new empty list named result.
syntaxExplanations.13: return hands result back here — an early exit.
syntaxExplanations.14: = assigns the value of start to current.
syntaxExplanations.15: while … : loops while the condition holds; != means 'not equal to'.
syntaxExplanations.16: links[current] reads the element of links at index current.
syntaxExplanations.17: == tests whether the candidate's value equals val.
syntaxExplanations.18: Copies one list element (links[nxt]) into another position (links[current]).
syntaxExplanations.19: Stores -1 into links at index nxt.
syntaxExplanations.20: Stores 1 into removed at index nxt.
syntaxExplanations.21: [current] is a one-element list holding the value of current.
syntaxExplanations.22: else: introduces the branch taken when the if condition above is false.
syntaxExplanations.23: = reassigns current to the value of nxt.
syntaxExplanations.24: Resets changed_links to an empty list.
syntaxExplanations.25: Creates a new empty list named result.
syntaxExplanations.26: = assigns the value of start to walk.
syntaxExplanations.27: while … : loops while walk is not equal to -1.
syntaxExplanations.28: .append(...) adds one item onto the end of the list result.
syntaxExplanations.29: Follows the link by reading links at index walk.
syntaxExplanations.30: return hands result back as the function's output.
